We do this because we do a lot of things around consumption. So we email twice a day. We have a ton of CRM follow-up. We do setters, all that. So you're just going to have your opt-in pages. Different ones with three different brand faces. Now and then we also have a fourth and a fifth that we just turned off. Then a DTA which is going to be your headline VSL application. Everything we do is over the phone. Okay, so that's how we did 70 plus million of what we did. And then we rolled all these funnels. We actually did 74 million through one funnel, which is just that core one up top. And again, opt-in. Right now we just do name and email, no phone. Land on a VSL page that's gated, that's basically cookie until it hits the application timing. Application. Then a setter is going to reach out to them and it's not a four-two call. Close— we're actually talking about doing that. And then we're also going to try the recruiter model method. So, uh, Jamie, uh, I'll let you share. Yeah, so we moved everyone from 1099 to W2. It was... This is not like me. It was eight, nine, 10% based on sales per booking. Sales per booking is a marketing business owner metric, not a salesperson metric. The second they get too many bookings or a reschedule or a no-show or a cancel, they're like, 'Can you deduct that single one?' And they hate it. They hate it. They hate it. But it is the most like clean and clear number for you as an owner to be like, 'Oh, if it's 15% plus, like I'm in the clear, which is really show rate times close rate, right?' So 50%, 30%, that gets you your 15. This is not like me. But so we changed from the tranches of that, basically taking them from eight, nine, ten, and then this massive bonus pool that was based on points. So basically, if they close during the week, it was one dollar per cash collected. And then, over the weekend, it was one point five X. So when it was doing those, they were hitting crazy bonuses like three six nine 12k in a pay period every other so like I had reps making like 40 plus G's on like less than 400k so like it— it netted out. Are you messing with me? Yeah, actually, I was. I was trying to make it better, but I made it worse. That's okay. Um, yeah, so in the end, uh, we moved it to a new model which Gentry likes a lot, which is, which is essentially six-eight-ten percent. I'm sorry, six-eight-eleven, and it's based on units. So now it's going to be based on how many units in the month close tranches. So, if you're 0 to 15 units, 15 to 30 units, 30 units plus, you hit different commission tiers. There's weekly bonuses that are competition-based and there's monthly bonuses and annual bonuses that are on total cash collected. So it basically is going to drag the average back down to like a 10 and some Aboná. So yeah, it's basically going to move it to where the blended average, because all I care about when I look at the system as a whole, you have a sales manager, you have a sales ops coordinator, you have closers, and you have setters. Right? You've got the Josh's of the world where if you outsource it, it's 20%. So in-house, your idea is what? 17% you want to keep it at? Yeah. Right. So mine is 23, 20. Not good. Right and W2 and all the fun and all that. So how do I get it back into the pocket? So we knew we were going to lose reps. We knew they were going to ask for their own custom pay plans. They've been here longer. All this kind of stuff. But this is going to incentivize a top closer to still make the same money they were making, but it's the middle and bottom that's gonna drag the actual net average down to where it's gonna be closer to 10.
